steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The notes give two numbers: all fifty state constitutions have voting rights clauses, but only thirty have free-elections clauses. The student wants to use these notes to make a claim about free-elections clauses specifically.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Although all states' constitutions have clauses conferring the right to vote, free and fair elections aren't mandated with the same consistency.’: correctly captures the 50-vs-30 contrast and applies it to a meaningful generalization about free-elections clauses. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘Every state constitution has some form of a free-elections clause, by which a state confers voting rights on eligible citizens.’: directly contradicts the notes (only 30 out of 50 do). ✗
  • Choice C — ‘States bear most of the responsibility for establishing voting rights and conducting fair elections.’: focuses on voting rights clauses without mentioning free-elections clauses at all, ignoring the key data point. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘In addition to constitutional provisions such as free-elections clauses, state lawmakers provide election guidance by means of legislation.’: shifts the focus to legislation rather than constitutional clauses, which is outside the scope of the notes. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
